analogy: Think of "Human Environment Settlement Transport and Communication" through the chapter's own opening images: In Chapter 5, you have seen that water means life to plants, animals and people. It is difficult for anyone to live in places where there is no water to drink, where there is no grass for their cattle to feed on and where there... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: THE HOT DESERT – SAHARA, 50 OUR ENVIRONMENT, LIFE IN THE DESERTS 51, 52 OUR ENVIRONMENT. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Human Environment Settlement Transport and Communication is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that in Chapter 5, you have seen that water means life to plants, animals and people.

The text explains that it is difficult for anyone to live in places where there is no water to drink, where there is no grass for their cattle to feed on and where there is no water to help the crops to grow.

The text explains that we will now learn about the places in the world where people have learned to cope with extreme harsh temperatures; in some places as hot as fire and some as cold as ice.

The text explains that these are characterised by low rainfall, scanty vegetation and extreme temperatures.

The text explains that depending on the temperatures there can be hot deserts or cold deserts.

The text explains that the people inhabit these lands wherever little water is available to practise agriculture.

The text explains that tHE HOT DESERT – SAHARA Look at the map of the world and the continent of Africa.

The text explains that locate the Sahara desert covering a large part of North Africa.

The text explains that it has an area of around 8.54 million sq. km.

The text explains that do you recall that India has an area of 3.28 million sq. km?

The text explains that these are Algeria, Chad, Egypt, Libya, Mali, Mauritania, Morocco, Niger, Sudan, Tunisia and Western Sahara.

The text explains that when you think of a desert the picture that immediately comes to your mind is that of sand.

The text explains that but besides the vast stretches of sands, that Sahara desert is covered with, there are also gravel plains and elevated plateaus with bare rocky surface.

The text explains that these rocky surfaces may be more than 2500m high at some places.

The text explains that 7.1: The Sahara Desert Glossary Desert: It is an arid region characteriesed by extremely high or low temperatures and has scarce vegetation.

The text explains that 7.2: Sahara in Africa Al Azizia in the Sahara desert, south of Tripoli, Libya recorded the highest temperature of 57.7°C in 1922.

The text explains that the temperatures during the day may soar as high as 50°C, heating up the sand and the bare rocks, which in turn radiates heat making everything around hot.

The text explains that the nights may be freezing cold with temperatures nearing zero degrees.

The text explains that other minerals of importance that are found in the area include iron, phosphorus, manganese and uranium.

The text explains that the day temperatures in summer are just above zero degree and the night temperatures well below –30°C.

The text explains that it is freezing cold in the winters when the temperatures may remain below –40°C for most of the time.

The text explains that drass, one of the coldest inhabited places on earth is located in Ladakh.

The text explains that due to scarcity of resources like water and fuel, they are used with reverence and care.

The text explains that questions such as - in what type of deserts it is found?

The text explains that explain to the class that they have to guess what animal matches the characteristics listed on the paper.

The text explains that 56 OUR ENVIRONMENT http://school.discovery.com/ http://nationalgeographic.com/ http://www.incredibleindia.org/ http://www.greenpeace.org/ http://www.britannica.com/ http;//www.animalplanet.co.uk/ Some Internet Sources for More Information APPENDIX

The text explains that the class can be divided in three to four groups.

The text explains that they will compete against each other in the ‘desert game’.

The text explains that each group now takes turn in guessing the correct answer.

The text explains that for example: • Animal of hot desert • Has double set of eyelashes to keep away the sand • The hide is used for making water bottles The correct answer is ‘camel’.

The text explains that within the group there will be a student who has prepared the card.

The text explains that ten points are awarded for the correct answer.

The text explains that this game will enable students to understand the desert.

The text explains that you can play the same game by taking different types of fruits, flora and the clothes the people wear.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Our Environment, Chapter 7: Human Environment Settlement Transport and Communication

prelims_traps: Do not treat THE HOT DESERT – SAHARA and 50 OUR ENVIRONMENT as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Human Environment Settlement Transport and Communication; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use THE HOT DESERT – SAHARA and 50 OUR ENVIRONMENT to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
